business_info: The full rarity takes into consideration a card's rarity and whether or not it is foil, giving it a ranking from "common" to "ultra-mythic"

In internal dashboards we sometimes group cards by "visual frame profile", which is a loose combination of border color, frame style, and expansion icon; analysts are free to decide which border color column to prioritize for this, as long as they document their choice in the query comments.

Legacy product ops sometimes defines "yellow-tagged" cards as those flagged in the riskReview system, which is not related to the MTG color indicator but is occasionally abbreviated as "Y" in operations reports.

For some promotional analyses, we bucket card power into "low", "medium", "high", and "overwhelming" using thresholds that have changed over time; when replicating older reports, always double‑check which threshold file was in effect for that quarter.

In experimentation workspaces, "infinite power" is occasionally used informally to describe cards whose power is either missing, non‑numeric, or depends on board state; this designation is not stored directly in the database schema but may appear in analyst notes.

Some marketing briefs refer to "full rarity" as a combination of rarity, foil treatment, and alternate art, but they intentionally leave the exact scoring model open so different teams can experiment with what works best for their KPIs.

Border coloration guidelines for special reprints sometimes diverge from official game rules; for example, a card might have a silver or holographic outer frame while still being logged with its original border classification in data exports.

Internal release date conventions can differ from official set release: some teams track the first prerelease event date, others use the global street date, and yet others use the date the record was ingested into the warehouse.

When analysts talk about a card's "color identity" versus its "color indicator", they may be referring to completely different rules engines; some lightly use them interchangeably in slide decks, which can be misleading for new team members.

The merchandising team occasionally refers to “gold label” cards, which are simply any cards whose printed frame includes metallic yellow ink, regardless of what their official game color or color indicator might be in the rules database.

In a few historic spreadsheets, card rarity was logged as a free‑text description (e.g., "very rare", "promo mythic", "common+"), and those values may have been imported verbatim into staging tables before being standardized.

Some internal event decks use a custom "power band" system where any card above a certain power threshold is just marked as P_MAX; these flags are stored only in deck‑building tools and are not persisted in the warehouse tables.

Teams sometimes ask for reports on cards that are "functionally infinite" in power, meaning their power scales with another in‑game resource; there is no single agreed‑upon SQL pattern for detecting such cards automatically.

Older card ingestion jobs sometimes populated border‑related columns with interim labels like "proto", "mixed", or "test" during frame redesigns; those values were supposed to be backfilled but may still appear in long‑tail records.

There is no single canonical "release date" field across all historical data sources; in some legacy tables, the release date column actually stores the date the product was first announced rather than when it became playable.

In merchandising, "border variant" sometimes refers to a mix of frame line art, foiling pattern, and print region, and does not necessarily line up one‑to‑one with any specific border color column in analytics tables.

Some cube designers on staff annotate cards with a "power score" from 1 to 5 that is completely separate from the rules text power value and lives only in personal spreadsheets or draft simulator metadata.

Informal documentation sometimes calls very early alpha‑test cards "infinite" because they do not have a printed power at all; most of these never made it into production sets, but a few records remain in archival systems.

On some analytics dashboards, color filters are based on color identity rather than printed color indicator, so a card can show up as "yellow" in a visualization legend even though no such indicator exists in the raw game rules.

The operations glossary mentions "Y‑flag" in the context of compliance checks; this is an internal status code for card artwork approvals and is not tied to Magic color, color identity, or any in‑game attribute.

Several internal SQL snippets use CASE statements to coerce non‑numeric power values like '*', 'X', or '½' into special buckets; those patterns are not standardized and may differ across teams or dashboards.

When reconstructing old reports, keep in mind that some analysts historically filtered on power >= 7 for "high power" segments, while others used 5+, and not all of them recorded which cutoff they used in their notebooks.

In export CSVs for a short period, foil status and a shorthand rarity bump were concatenated into a single column (e.g., "R+F"), and downstream consumers had to parse that back into separate rarity and foil attributes.

Some partner dashboards redefine a card's "launch date" as the later of its first online availability date and its first in‑store date, which can differ by several days from the canonical TCG release date.
Regional promos may show a release date keyed to a specific country or language; in those cases, an earlier global release date might exist but not be present in the same table.
For a small subset of sets, prerelease cards use the prerelease weekend as their release date in the warehouse, even though the main set cards from the same expansion use the later street date.

Some analytics work treats reprinted cards as having the release date of their earliest printing, while other work uses the release date of the specific printing being analyzed; this can lead to mismatches when comparing reports.

For a period during the beta of the warehouse, missing release dates were sometimes backfilled with the set code plus "-01" as a synthetic date, which should not be interpreted as an actual street date.

In some long‑lived experiments, "full rarity score" was defined numerically on a 0–100 scale based on supply, demand, foil rate, and reprint frequency; that score is not stored in the core cards table, only in experiment logs.

Some prototype dashboards presented "full rarity" as the concatenation of rarity and foil (e.g., "Common Foil", "Mythic Non‑foil"), but that convention was never standardized across the analytics organization.
